data.title: Back To Google
data.description:
There's a popular meme on Twitter that goes like this:

"I'm starting to be a real believer in AI. Will be a megatrend like no one is really imagining right now. I'm sure lots of ramps and drops along the way, though"

Like any good meme, there is indeed an ounce of truth to it. Maybe not an ounce; I actually think there is likely to be a lot of truth to it. However, as former Bernstein Internet Analyst Corry Wang (who is incidentally currently at Google AI Strategy team) put it : "There is a misguided obsession in many tech circles around predicting the future of technology. In contrast, I would posit that predicting the future is actually pretty easy - the hard part is making any money on it."

Indeed, a couple of years ago, it was somewhat easy to foresee that the narrative around the future of search would likely continue to sour. It did, but the details have been somewhat more difficult to be right about. For example, since the rise of GenAI and chatbots, one common concern has been a potential double whammy for incumbents such as Google (NASDAQ:GOOG)(NASDAQ:GOOGL), i.e. GenAI queries are harder to monetize and at the same time, the cost to serve such queries would be materially higher than traditional search queries. I doubt anyone was expecting Google's operating margin would go higher, from 30.6% in 2021 to 32.1% in 2024. I myself thought Google Services operating margin in 2021 might be "peak margin" for Google as they would have to navigate much higher cost to serve queries and lower monetization phenomenon (admittedly, part of this margin expansion was due to extending depreciation schedule, but even if you adjust that, 2021 and 2024 Google Services operating margin would be somewhat similar). What actually happened was Google reduced cost to serve these GenAI queries by 90% in 18 months through "hardware, engineering and technical breakthroughs" (3Q'24 call). Moreover, Google has stumbled onto new query markets e.g. in 4Q'24 call, they mentioned how lens is used for 20 billion visual search queries, majority of which are incremental. How about monetization of these GenAI queries? It's less reassuring so far, but in 1Q'24 call Google management mentioned "based on our testing so far, I'm comfortable and confident that we'll be able to manage the monetization transition here well as well."

This isn't a Deep (or even shallow) Dive on Google, so I'm not going to focus much on numbers or try to address/discuss all the Google related concerns (read this piece if you're more interested in numbers; I may also do an update later in the year).

Look, when I sold Google in December 2023, I did mention I expect search to go through a significant transformation in the next 10-15 years. It's always dangerous to change your mind on something in two years that you expect to play out over a much longer period. I still have plenty of sympathies for search related concerns. And even from a personal perspective, while it is true that Google did lose my query share mostly to OpenAI, Google hardly lost any of my monetizable query share. However, if you want to disrupt Google, you always were going to disrupt the non-monetizable queries first and then work your way to monetizable queries later. So, I am certainly not claiming Google to be out of the woods yet. My appreciation of the risk is certainly why I made Google only a 3% position so far, with an average cost of $150. I will pay close attention to price and broader developments in AI before sizing it up more.

However, I do want to mention a couple of things to give you a sense of what prompted to change my mind.

Let me be very frank. When I sold Google in 2023, I hardly knew much about semiconductors, and I knew even less about TPUs. As I gained a bit more understanding over the last 15 months, I started appreciating there are certainly scenarios in which Google's deep infrastructure advantage and breadth of existing relationship with users can mask almost all of their weaknesses. I have been following big tech since 2018-19 fairly closely, and it never becomes easier to predict how the narratives will shift just in 2-3 years down the line. Just imagine trying to predict the narrative around Meta today back in 2022 (or "Apple 2021 narrative" back in 2018). I certainly do not rule out the possibility that search can chug along just fine, and thanks to Google's almost end to end control over their infrastructure (unfortunately, they still do have dependency on TSMC for fabricating TPUs), Google's narrative can change materially especially in the post-regulatory clarity era.

I am not super confident in predicting how the big debates will be settled in AI, but it does seem there is a distinct scenario in which Google can potentially be materially ahead of everyone else in 5 years. Outlining the exact path is hard to do, but I have come to the view that while the search related debates consume all the airtime, most investors are potentially missing or underappreciating a scenario in which Google may just topple everyone else by leveraging their infrastructure advantage.

But isn't Google pretty bad at productizing their AI advancements? They have already deployed quite impressive models and capabilities, but if they're bad at productizing it, will this infrastructure advantage matter at all?

While I was pondering about the point about infrastructure advantage for almost a year now, the recent Sharp Tech podcast episode really drove the point home and also reminded me why Google may be able to succeed despite its weakness in productizing model capabilities. A listener of the pod sent the following musing for Ben and Andrew:

"When exactly was Google good at building new products? The answer is never. Google has always sucked at building new products. Consider the epic failures of Google Buzz, Wave and Plus or G chat, and Google Hangouts.

Google was only ever successful at innovating and building one of the following three things:

  • Google search
  • A critical infrastructure required and custom tailored for Google search.
  • Products where search-like characteristics, huge scale, and a data flywheel turned out to be critical for success.

Google pioneered amazing concepts such as map reduce, batch processing, zero trust security, containers, software-defined networking to name a few. However, it sucked when it came to wrapping them as general purpose infrastructure products. This goes back to the famous 2011 Stevey's platform rant.

Stevey's platform rant predicted so well why Google Cloud Provider was destined to lag behind AWS. This is also why, despite employing a phenomenal collection of talent with security researchers and engineers, Google could never have built a product like Wiz for external use. All of this is to say, I don't think there's anything new going on. Maybe this is just Google being Google, the awkward nerd that gains his advantage by staying up in the data center all night figuring out how to stack 20 times more servers compared to Inktomi who occupied the cage next door. Of course, OpenAI has the better polished product. That was never Google's advantage. And if that's what it takes to win an AI, then well, Google is just not going to win. Not now and not ever in its history.

The only hope is if it turns out AI models aren't a commodity. Search engines were considered a commodity back in the Inktomi, Alta Vista days, very much like LLMs today. Google's only hope is if, just like in the early 2000s, it turns out the consensus is wrong and there is a sustainable long-term advantage to be gained by better infrastructure engineering."

But is this a good time to buy Google when majority of the revenue is largely dependent on advertising revenue, given the recently introduced tariffs may even cause a global recession?

Tariffs are certainly a risk, but let me offer some brief thoughts on them. Let me provide a historical analogy that I think can have some resemblance to today. Imagine we are in late 2001 to early 2002. Tech bubble just crashed and people just experienced 9/11. I bet 9/11 consumed almost all of our attention (and rightly so), and perhaps more and more people started laughing at people who thought internet would revolutionize everything. In my mind, these tariffs (if they remain unchanged) are like 9/11 i.e. incredibly impactful for the world and will certainly affect us in ways we may not be realizing today, just as 9/11 did. But from purely long-term market perspective, 9/11 wasn't the main story that mattered; what mattered was the internet.

Today's "internet" is "Generative AI". In a decade (likely lot sooner), it is much more likely than not that Gen AI will be the dominant driver of the market, and while tariffs may be impactful in the meantime, they are mostly going to be adapted and absorbed by different stakeholders in the value chain.

Google, Meta (META), and Amazon (AMZN) have all been down 30% from their highs. I have started deploying my capital to all of them today (3% Google at $150, and adding more to my existing Amazon and Meta positions: 1% Amazon at $168, 1% Meta at $500). I am keeping an eye on Microsoft as well, but haven't pulled the trigger yet. I expect myself to buy more if stocks keep going down. There is a very good probability that people may "forget" about AI amidst the tariff tantrum, but I am of the opinion that might lead to a compelling opportunity to steer my portfolio to the future of the world even more.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: ECH: Hopes On November Elections Might Lead To High Gains At High Risk
data.description:
Introduction

Chile is a country that might be overlooked by many investors. This article will show why the iShares MSCI Chile ETF (BATS:ECH) might still be a good investment for risk-seeking investors due to the chance of a new government during this year's elections.

Key ETF Data

ECH currently has a TER of 0.6%, while it is expected to pay out a dividend of 0.56%. Fundamentally, the fund trades at a P/E ratio of 11.91 while having a book ratio of only 1.27, making it cheap. At over 300k daily traded shares, the ETF also has good liquidity.

Figure 1 shows the holdings breakdown of ECH. Overall, the ETF is only minimally diversified, with Financials accounting for 30% of all holdings. Additionally, three other sectors (Basic Material, Consumer Cyclicals, and Utilities) make up more than 10% of the portfolio each.

The concentration risk of ECH becomes even more obvious when looking at single positions, as displayed in Figure 2. Here, Banco De Chile accounts for almost 13% of the ETF, while other positions like Banco Santander Chile are significantly overweight as well. At only 31 holdings and more than 65% exposure in the top 10 holdings, ECH can be seen as very risky.

Economic Conditions

Currently, Chile shows solid GDP growth at 4%, which is the best value since Q2 2022. Nevertheless, it does not remotely touch levels of over 15% yearly GDP growth during 2021. Meanwhile, inflation is still elevated at 4.7%, higher than the pre-2022 levels of around 3%. This is especially a problem as there has been a visible uptrend in inflation over the past year. The same goes for unemployment which is elevated at 8.4% and in an uptrend as well. Concerning interest rates, they are currently at 5% and have continuously been cut over the past 1.5 years. The fact that we have currently seen the longest period of no movement in interest rates might suggest, however, that the cutting cycle is over. Overall, the outlook looks rather neutral as the GDP grows despite rising unemployment. The fact that inflation is slightly rising might further suggest that interest rates will be kept at current levels for some time. This is further supported by a low debt/GDP ratio of 39.4% and a trade surplus, suggesting that Chile does not depend on elevated inflation.

Interestingly, Chile's biggest trading partner is China, accounting for 35% of Chile's exports and 25% of its imports. Meanwhile, the country's second-biggest partner is the US, at 13% of exports. This, combined with the fact that more than half of Chile's GDP depends on trading, makes the country dependent on global trade, which currently is very uncertain.

Political Analysis

Currently, Chile is governed by the left-wing coalition Apruebo Dignidad which emphasizes wealth redistribution and nationalization of lithium mining. This makes it very risky to invest in Chile as nationalization is a nightmare for equity owners. However, in current polls, the UDI, a center-right party, is leading, pushing for less immigration and a more conservative government. Should the party further gain momentum, this would be very bullish for Chilean stocks as they push for free-market economics and limited government intervention. Also, the party names attracting foreign capital as a goal. The election will take place on November 16 this year.

Technical Perspective

From a technical perspective, ECH does look very attractive at the moment. The ETF has gained more than 12% on both a 6-month and 1-year time frame. Additionally, it is up ytd in contrast to US markets and trades well above its 200d moving average. Furthermore, the ETF has a correlation to the S&P500 of only 59%, making it a possible diversifier.

Chances and Risks

As I have already said, ECH is a very risky investment. It is more volatile than traditional stocks, and the country is still reigned by a left-wing party that might still nationalize companies before the elections at the end of the year. In my opinion, the performance of ECH will likely depend more on politics and polls than on the actual economy at the moment since interest rates are likely to stagnate at the current level of 5%. This makes a shift in public opinion a big risk factor. The same goes for global trade, which currently faces a rather pessimistic outlook amidst the tariff policies of President Trump. Nevertheless, the currently low valuations of ECH make it a viable option with big chances should we see a change of government at the end of the year.

Conclusion

As the analysis has shown, there are many arguments in favor of ECH as well as against it. In my opinion, there is a solid profit opportunity that matches the current high risk of an investment in the region. Because of this, I give ECH a buy rating that is closer to hold than to strong buy. Nevertheless, it should be emphasized that this is a high-risk investment and nothing for conservative investors.

data.title: IBM: Is The Stock Price Overvalued?
data.description:
By Anthony Goh, Senior Investment Research Analyst @ Khaveen Investments

In our previous analysis of International Business Machines Corporation (NYSE:IBM), we believed its strategies in AI Innovation for Enterprise, Software-focused Growth, and Cross-selling could drive sustained growth. We believed IBM's watsonx AI solutions would accelerate enterprise adoption through strategic partnerships, while hybrid cloud would be the primary growth driver, supported by a 17% CAGR. We also saw the Software segment’s 4.1% average growth indicative of continued momentum. Since our previous analysis, IBM’s stock has surged by 36%, surpassing our 19% upside target, leading us to examine whether the company may be overvalued. We first analyze its valuation using comparable multiples against competitors. Next, we assess its valuation based on historical multiples. We then review our DCF analysis for the company, updating our growth and profitability assumptions, and then determining which valuation method is most appropriate.

Comparable Valuation Ratios

In the first section, we compare IBM’s valuation ratios including P/E, P/S and P/FCF with competitors. We compiled a total of 54 companies, the top 10 from IT Consulting, the top 30 from Software and the top 14 from Tech Hardware, and calculated the weighted average based on IBM’s revenue breakdown to compare against the company. We also compiled the 5-year average net income, revenue and FCF growth.

From the table, we see that the Software industry has much higher multiples (48.48x P/E, 14.41x P/S, and 65.24x P/FCF), whereas IT Consulting and Tech Hardware show lower valuation, we believe one factor is the higher revenue, net income and FCF growth compared to IT Consulting and Tech Hardware.

Comparing the weighted average for the valuation ratios, IBM’s P/E ratio is about is fairly in line, only 0.42% lower than the weighted average and its P/S and P/FCF ratios much lower at 58.4% and 47.8% below the weighted averages respectively, which indicates IBM being undervalued.

But when examining the revenue, net income and FCF growth, we see that IBM has much lower 5-year average growth compared to the industry weighted averages with net income at -8.58% vs 16.69% (25% lower), revenue at 1.69% vs 12.60% (11% lower), and free cash flow at -13.00% vs 20.08% (33% lower), showing that IBM has been underperforming its peers.

We also plotted the Software, IT Consulting and Tech Hardware valuation ratios against the average growth for revenue, net income and FCF. The charts generally show that commonly across all 3 industries, there is a rising trend, indicating the higher the growth, the higher the valuation ratio. There are several outliers though, such as Apple in Tech Hardware with high multiples despite low revenue, income and FCF growth.

In the table above, we calculated a weighted average difference based on IBM’s segment breakdown and the difference between its valuation ratios with the trendline, where we calculated a weighted average difference of 4% lower for IBM compared to the P/S trendline but 55.8% and 58% higher than the trendline for P/E and P/FCF, which indicates it may be overvalued in terms of P/E and P/FCF but not in terms of P/S.

Based on our comparison of the company’s valuation ratios against competitors across Software, IT Consulting, and Tech Hardware, we initially found that its P/E and P/FCF ratios were lower by 58.40% and 47.76%, respectively. We believe one factor contributing to these lower ratios is the company’s weaker growth relative to competitors, particularly in net profit and FCF, which have been negative on a five-year average basis. After deriving trendlines across the industries in which IBM operates, we observed that its P/E and P/FCF ratios exceed the weighted average by 55.8% and 58%, suggesting the company is overvalued rather than undervalued in these metrics.

But in terms of P/S, the weighted average difference to the trendline is only 4%, which does not indicate overvaluation. This comparison between IBM and competitors was based on its historical growth rates, where its earnings and FCF negative average growth were influenced by factors such as in 2022 where it had a one-off non-cash pension settlement charge of $5.9 bln as well as a $2.9 bln one time tax benefit due to its acquisition of Red Hat in 2019. Excluding these one-off adjustments, we calculated its average net income would be -0.1% which is still lower than the weighted average of 16.7%. As we cannot conclude the company is overvalued in terms of relative valuation to competitors alone, we examine its historical valuation ratios next.

Historical Valuation Ratios

We look at IBM's historical valuation ratios, including P/S, P/E and P/FCF, to analyze the trend and compare its current ratio with its historical ratio. We compiled the company’s ratios in the past 5 years, as well as its growth performance by revenue, net income and FCF by year.

The company's valuation ratios have generally been on a rising trend. P/S has increased each year, and revenue growth improved from 2020 to 2022 before slowing in 2023 and 2024. Its current P/S ratio is higher than its 5-year average. The analyst consensus for 5-year average revenue growth is 4.7%, indicating expectations of recovery, which management has also continued to highlight in its recent earnings briefing. In terms of earnings, its growth has been volatile but the current P/E ratio is fairly in line with its 5-year average while its P/FCF ratio has been increasing steadily as FCF growth improved (29% in 2024), the highest in the past five years.

The company’s overall current valuation ratios compared with the company’s historical 5-year averages show the company may be overvalued due to the large differences between 15% for its PE to 60% for its P/S ratio, but we believe one of the factors could be the expectations of its revenue growth to recover and its FCF growth trend which has been increasing. We also calculated the company’s 1-yr forward P/S, P/E and P/FCF valuation ratio based on analysts’ consensus, which is 3.64x, 27.3x and 19x respectively, while the difference between these ratios and IBM’s historical average is lower than compared with the current ratios, it is still a difference of 52.91%, 7.88% and 47.48%, indicating it may be overvalued.

We believe the differences between the current valuation ratios and historical ratios indicate the company may be overvalued. IBM’s revenue growth is expected to recover and its FCF growth has been accelerating but we believe, as its forward multiples are even higher than its historical average, that the main reason for the rising trend of IBM’s valuation ratios is due to the Tech sector’s rising valuation multiples in the past 5 years, such as the Tech sector’s P/E of 22.66x in Jan 2022 to 36x in Dec 2024. In our previous analysis, we highlighted that we expected the company’s revenue growth to accelerate driven by growth of the Software segment supported by its hybrid cloud business at a CAGR of 17%, due to the company’s strength with watsonx and expected synergies between hybrid cloud and consulting. This translates to an overall forecasted revenue growth of 6% for 2025. In the next section, we will focus on the company’s FCF growth and update our valuation of the company.

DCF Valuation

In the last section, as we highlighted the company’s improving FCFs, we analyze it further in this section and update our valuation based on its discounted cash flows.

In terms of the company’s FCF margin analysis above, its cash from operations % of revenue had been fairly stable in 2024. Its higher FCF margin in 2024 was mainly due to lower other investing cash flows compared to 2023 at 5.4% of revenue instead of 9.4% in the previous year as the company had lower spending on M&A at $5 bln compared to $3.8 in the previous year, such as Apptio Inc ($4.6 bln). IBM’s capex % of revenue has been decreasing over the past 10 years, at 2.4% in 2024, which we believe reflects the low capital intensity of software businesses, as its segment revenue grew to a higher share of revenues. IBM’s FCF margin based only on capex was stable in 2024 at 21% and has been positive in all of the past 10 years.

We updated our DCF valuation, maintaining similar revenue growth rate projections from our previous analysis, driven by its Software segment and hybrid cloud growth at a 5-year forward average of 5.2%. We updated our COGS % of revenue assumption to decrease at a rate of 2% based on its historical 10-year average as the company’s software segment, which has higher margins, grows faster than the remaining segments. Based on its annual report, its Software segment, which includes hybrid cloud, has a profit margin of 32.1%, higher than its Consulting (9.9%) and Infrastructure (17.5%) segments. For its capex, we modeled it to decline to 1.8% of revenue, as we expect its capex % of revenue trend to continue decreasing due to the faster growth of its Software business, which is also more in line with its 5-year average Capex % of revenue (1.7%).

We also updated its discount rate based on WACC, which is now slightly higher at 7.6% compared to 6.8%. For terminal value, we retain its EV/EBITDA based on a 5-year average updated to 18.24x, slightly higher due to its rising valuation ratio over the past year.

Based on these updated assumptions, our DCF model shows IBM is not overvalued, rather it still has a further upside of around 19%, as we anticipate higher profitability margin growth, with an average forward projected net profit margin of 14.2% compared to 9.6% in 2024, as well as moderating capex % of revenue due to the higher growth of its Software business segment which, we believe, has lower capital intensity due to the software business model. In terms of our selection to base our valuation for IBM, we believe the DCF valuation method is more appropriate compared to our valuation ratio analysis in the first and second sections, mainly due to it being forward-looking unlike in the first section where we compared IBM to competitors based on historical growth, and our DCF spans a 10-years projection, accounting for changes in its business model as its Software segment growth outpaces the growth of its other segments and grows to a larger share of its revenues, unlike in the second point where we examined the 5-yr average analysts’ consensus.

Risk: Competition in Hybrid Cloud

We believe one of the risks for IBM is competition in the cloud market, while the company specifically focuses on the hybrid cloud segment, larger cloud players like AWS (AMZN), Google Cloud (GOOG), and Microsoft Azure (MSFT) are also expanding in this segment, which we see a threat for IBM to scale its meager 2% cloud market share. For example, AWS Outposts extends AWS to on-premises, while Storage Gateway links local apps to AWS storage whereas Google Cloud Anthos manages apps across clouds and on-premises.

Verdict

By plotting the growth of companies in the tech industries against valuation ratios, we see from our derived trendlines that IBM’s P/E and P/FCF ratios exceed the weighted average by 55.8% and 58%, but this is based on the company’s historical performance. We continue to expect an improvement in IBM going forward due to its higher growth Software segment supporting overall company growth and stronger profitability margins. We also compared IBM’s current valuation ratios with its historical averages, which also suggest possible overvaluation at current levels when compared with its historical averages, but we believe expected revenue recovery and improving FCF growth may justify its higher P/S multiple. In our updated DCF model, a forward 5-year average revenue growth of 5.2%, driven supported by hybrid cloud expansion, watsonx AI, and synergies between hybrid cloud and consulting, as well as higher 5-year forward average net margins of 14.2%, we see a 19% upside for IBM, rating it as a Buy. We consider our DCF model the most reflective of IBM's value and we increase our price target of IBM to $299.57 from $217.70 previously, accounting for higher margin growth and terminal value based on 5-year average EV/EBITDA.

data.title: Employment Report: 228K Jobs Added In March, More Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest employment report showed that 228,000 jobs were added in March, exceeding the expected 137,000 addition. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate unexpectedly inched up to 4.2%.

Here is an excerpt from the Employment Situation Summary released on Friday morning by the Bureau of Labor Statistics:

Total nonfarm payroll employment rose by 228,000 in March, and the unemployment rate changed little at 4.2 percent, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported today. Job gains occurred in health care, in social assistance, and in transportation and warehousing. Employment also increased in retail trade, partially reflecting the return of workers from a strike. Federal government employment declined.

Household Survey Data: Both the unemployment rate, at 4.2 percent, and the number of unemployed people, at 7.1 million, changed little in March. The unemployment rate has remained in a narrow range of 4.0 percent to 4.2 percent since May 2024.

Establishment Survey Data: Total nonfarm payroll employment rose by 228,000 in March, higher than the average monthly gain of 158,000 over the prior 12 months. In March, job gains occurred in health care, in social assistance, and in transportation and warehousing. Employment also increased in retail trade, partially reflecting the return of workers from a strike. Federal government employment declined.

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in nonfarm employment over the last four years. The 3-month moving average is currently at 152,000, down from the previous month.

For another view, here is the monthly percent change in nonfarm employment since 2000. We've added a 12-month moving average to highlight the long-term trend. The latest 12-month moving average is at 157,000, down from the previous month.

Unemployment, Recessions, and Market Trends

The next chart illustrates the relationship between unemployment, recessions, and the S&P Composite since 1948. Unemployment is typically a lagging indicator that moves inversely to equity prices (the top series in the chart).

Notice the rising unemployment peaks in 1971, 1975, and 1982, which coincided with bear markets. A similar pattern briefly emerged during the COVID pandemic, but the impact was short-lived as irrational exuberance quickly took over.

The latest unemployment rate stands at 4.152% (to three decimal places).

Now, let's examine the unemployment rate as a recession indicator - specifically, the cyclical troughs in the unemployment rate (UR). The next chart highlights a 12-month moving average of the UR, with its troughs marked.

Currently, the unemployment rate stands at 4.15%, slightly below the latest 12-month moving average of 4.10%. As shown in the inset table, the correlation between these moving average troughs and the start of recessions is remarkably strong.

The most recent trough occurred 21 months ago, in June 2023, when the 12-month moving average of the unemployment rate fell to its lowest level since January 1970.

The next chart highlights the unemployment rate for those unemployed for 27 weeks or more. This rate has declined significantly from its all-time peak of 4.4% in April 2010. Following the COVID pandemic, it rose as high as 2.6% but has since trended downward. It now stands at 0.9%, unchanged from the previous month.

How long does unemployment typically last? As the next chart shows, the latest data indicates that the average duration of unemployment is 22.8 weeks. Historically, this metric tends to rise during and after recessions.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics’ broadest measure of unemployment, the U6 series, captures not only the unemployed but also the underemployed, marginally attached workers, and those who have stopped looking for work.

Often referred to as the 'real' unemployment rate, many economists consider it the most comprehensive gauge of labor market conditions. In contrast, the more widely cited unemployment rate only includes those who are unemployed and actively job-seeking within the past four weeks.

The U6 series currently stands at 7.9%, down from the previous month.

Labor Market Participation and Structural Shifts

For a broader perspective on the structural shifts in the U.S. labor market, the next chart illustrates the labor force participation rate. This metric measures the percentage of the civilian population aged 16 and over who are either employed or actively seeking work.

It provides a key estimate of an economy’s active workforce and serves as an important indicator of labor market health. Currently, the rate stands at 62.5%, up from the previous month.

Here’s another chart that provides a different perspective on the unemployment rate - unemployment divided by the labor force participation rate. This metric currently stands at 6.6%, unchanged from the previous month.

The next chart overlays the unemployment rate with the employment-population ratio, which measures the number of employed people as a percentage of the total civilian population aged 16 and over. The inverse correlation between the two series is clear.

The data also highlights the accelerating growth of women in the workforce and the rise of two-income households in the early 1980s. Before the COVID pandemic, the employment-population ratio fluctuated between 54.9% and 64.7%.

The lower end of this range dates back to the Eisenhower era - a time of predominantly one-income households, the Korean War, and the novelty of soft drinks being sold in cans.

In April 2020, the global pandemic drove the employment-population ratio to a record low of 51.2%. Since then, it has been gradually recovering toward its pre-pandemic range, with the latest reading at 59.9%, unchanged from the previous month.

Looking ahead, both the employment-population ratio and labor force participation rate will be key metrics to watch. The first wave of baby boomers continues to exert downward pressure on these figures. The oldest boomers became eligible for early retirement at the onset of the Great Recession, and their transition to full retirement age will continue through 2030.

Notes: The start date of 1948 in the charts above was determined by the earliest monthly employment data collected by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The best source for the historical data is the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Lavras Gold: On My Watch List As A Possible Future Acquisition
data.description:
Introduction

Lavras Gold Corp (TSX.V:LGC) (TSXV:LGC:CA) (OTCQX:LGCFF) is an exploration and development company operating in Brazil in an area known as Rio Grande do Sul. Lavras Gold is focused on its interests close to the town of Lavras Do Sul via 37 mineral rights across a land mass of 23,400 hectares as shown on the map below:

Historically, this is an interesting area for mining gold, and it has been going on since 1776 when the Portuguese pioneers discovered gold there. You would think that by now the area would have been mined out, but as time goes on, technology improves enabling further exploration

Lavras Gold Corp : Brief Description

In the few short years that Lavras Gold has been prospecting in this area they have made some interesting discoveries as tabulated on the chart below:

The above tabulation shows that the Butia discovery has 378,000/Ozs of gold on a Measured and Indicated basis and 115,000/Ozs of gold on an Inferred basis. And the Cerito discovery has 293,000/Ozs of gold on an Inferred basis. There are other discoveries which indicate the presence of gold along with copper and silver, which are also valuable commodities. My interest is tweaked as the possibility of going the distance with some sort of development here or becoming a target for one of the cash rich senior mining companies exists. Almost all of the household names in the gold mining space are currently involved in one form or another in Brazil and as their mines are depleted a suitable replacement will need to be acquired and Lavras may be one such target under consideration.

Financials

The above tabulation is a nice segway into their Financials as the next chart plots the effect on the stock price that some of these discoveries have had:

These discoveries appear to have been received fairly well by the investment community, as the price jumps indicate. Their top priority is to demonstrate that there is sufficient potential to develop an economically feasible gold mine via their exploratory findings. In order to finance this activity, they have a 2% revenue-based income from a gold royalty on a portfolio of exploration concessions close to the Mara Rosa Mine, which is located in central Brazil.

The following tabulation shows the company’s financial position and the standout for me and something to be aware of is that the deficit has increased to $7,637,016.

This deficit is referred to by the independent auditor who notes that “these events and conditions indicate that a material uncertainty exists that may cast significant doubt on the Company’s ability to continue as a going concern”

It is important in my view to be cognizant of such a risk when considering an investment of this nature.

However, I would also draw your attention to the “Management and Discussion And Analysis” for the year ending 31 December 2025 where under the heading of Highlights they state the following:

In early 2025, Lavras Gold raised C$15,002,900 at C$2.20 per common share through a brokered arrangement with several brokerage companies. These funds are sufficient to support the planned work programs at the LDS Project throughout 2025. In September 2023, the Company had raised C$13.7 million at C$1.35 per common share through a non-brokered private placement. No warrants were issued with either financing that Lavras Gold has completed since its inception in April 2022.

From the financial position statement above we can see that in 2023 the cash and cash equivalents stood at $11,052,838. That position in 2024 stood at $1,649,592 so as a rough estimate we can see that they spent approximately $10 million over that 12 month. So, having raised approximately $15 million in early 2025 this should be sufficient to cover their operations at the current rate of burn. Of course, this can change substantially if there was a sudden increase in the number of drilling rigs used or some other operational changes that have a cost impact, but for now, it looks as though they are covered financially for the year 2025.

This tells me that they have been able to go to the market and raise sufficient funds to continue with their endeavors throughout 2025. It is often the case that companies involved in exploration need to demonstrate that they indeed do have the makings of a future gold mine in order to raise more capital in order to complete the next phase of the project. Looking ahead, they may need to come to the market again to raise more capital in the years’ time. This, of course, could lead to the dilution of shares for the current investors. Lavras is also looking at their 2% revenue-based income stream with the view to leveraging this position to increase cash flow. At the moment, I don't know how they would achieve this and only time will tell what they decide to do regarding refinancing. However, it is something to keep an eye on though as future financial statements are issued.

Taking a quick look at Seeking Alphas database, we can glean the following: On a forward-looking basis, Lavras Gold Corp has an EPS (FWD) -0.06, a 52-week range of $1.36 to $2.89, and a market capitalization of $118.69M, but it is not covered by Quants or SA Analysts. However, Wall Street has it as a ‘Buy’ with a rating of 4.00

Lavras Gold Corp is traded on both the NYSE under the ticker symbol of OTCQX:LGCFF and in Canada under the symbol of TSX.V:LGC

Lavras Gold Corp One Year Progress Chart

Lavras Gold Corp From $1.50 to a peak of $2.85 and back to $1.99 all in one year, so not a stock for the faint-hearted. Although that can be said for a number of stocks in the precious metals space.

The technical indicators are heading south suggesting further softening of the stock price. The 50dma crossing over the 200dma in a downward motion suggests a weaker price in the near term. However, it should be noted that a lot depends on the news flow and the quality of the results going forward.

Stock Comparison Chart

The following chart compares Lavras Gold Corp with three other stocks chosen because they have similar market capitalizations, their progress over the last 12 months has been plotted and as we can see Lavras has gained 37.24%, which is less than the other three stocks that I've chosen for this comparison, but of course these things can change dramatically depending on which stocks you are comparing it to.

However a 37% increase in the share price over a 12-month period is not too shabby

A Quick Look At The Chart Of Gold Prices

A quick look at the gold chart as gold is critical to this company’s fortunes shows that gold is still north. Should the gold bull market continue then I would imagine that most of the associated stocks in this sector would benefit from such a rally, including Lavras Gold.

However our quest is to seek out those stocks that will outperform the sector average which makes it difficult for me to rate this stock as a Buy at this moment in time.

Conclusion

If I owned shares in this company I would be tempted to hold on to them for now and therefore will Lavras Gold Corp a ‘Hold’ rating.

However, I am not going to make an acquisition at this time but I will keep them on my Watch List and observe how they perform in the near term.

If you can find the time your comments are very much appreciated by our readers and myself so please fire them in, and I will do my best to address each and every one of them.

Go gently.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Massive Moat And Beta, Still A Buy
data.description:
Authored by Winfred Mwiti

Investment Summary

Since our last bullish article on Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) on September 10, 2024, the stock has dipped more than 15% underperforming the S&P 500.

In this follow-up analysis, we are reiterate our Buy rating. But the rating comes with some concern. Although SMCI's explosive revenue growth, AI infrastructure positioning and resilient customer base point to an optimistic view, the company’s governance and accounting concerns, margin pressure, and volatility warrant a cautious approach here.

Our Bullish Take: SMCI’s AI-Driven Ascension

1. Stellar Revenue Growth

Among SMCI's investment appeals is its strong revenue growth. In its 2Q25 earnings report, the company registered net sales in the region of $5.7 billion marking a YoY growth of 54%. As such, the current growth figure is above its 5-year average revenue growth rate of ~45% and the sector median YoY growth rate of ~5%.

Also, the last few quarters has seen triple digit revenue growth, marking a period of significant momentum for SMCI. This growth trajectory has been fueled by the strong demand for AI products particularly with the transition from Hopper to Blackwell GPUs expected to sustain demand.

With the global data center GPU market estimated to grow by a CAGR of 28.5% between 2024-2030, we expect this company to sustain its strong revenue growth momentum.

We believe that this bright market outlook justifies the upbeat consensus revenue estimates for FY2025 and FY2026 which are reflective of the company’s strong growth momentum.

2. SMCI AI Positioning And Market Expansion

Globally, the demand for AI-driven technologies is surging dramatically, with AI spending expected to grow to above 52% of the IT budget. Super Micro, being a key player in the ecosystem, is aligning itself to leverage the wave for its sustainable development. Recently, the company unveiled over 20 single socket servers and the industry's first 1U server which is powered by Nvidia’s Grace CPU Superchip (144 cores up to 1 petabyte of storage) thus underscoring its innovative edge. This being the first of its kind positions SMCI as a leading force in the data center transformation, signaling the pivotal role it's playing in the evolving industry. From where we sit, this market leadership is a key competitive advantage that positions the company in a position to seize market opportunities early through its superior product offering.

Furthermore, Super Micro is striving to expand its capacity to meet the global demand for AI and HPC solutions. Following recent expansions, the company can now deliver 5,000 air cooled/ 2,000 liquid cooled racks per month including advanced liquid-cooled solutions. In addition, the company is also expanding to high growth markets such as 5G and edge computing. For example, SMCI launched an edge computing server that features the latest Intel Xeon 6 processor offering up to 2.5x core count and improved performance per watt when compared to previous generations.

The company’s expansion extends facilities in Taiwan, the Netherlands, US and Malaysia which speaks about its geographical diversification as in its growth focus. It is our take that this company’s geographical diversification (serving more than 100 countries) coupled with improved production capacity mitigates supply chain risks. By operating facilities in multiple countries, The company reduces its reliance on a single region hence managing the impact of regional disruptions or localized supply chain bottlenecks. Similarly, its improved production capacity enhances its ability to meet its surging demand as well as compensate for delays in different regions.

3. SMCI's Economic Moat

We believe this SMCI's market position, more specifically its uniqueness is a key pillar to our bullish case. Its market strength lies in its role as a provider of customizable high-performance server solutions tailored for data centers, cloud computing as well as AI workloads. Unlike some competitors who offer standardized off-the-shelf products, SMCI emphasis is in its modular architectural approach, a flexibility that allows it to deliver bespoke solutions that meet the specific needs of its customers in the AI server market where demand for optimized hardware is surging. An important element of its uniqueness is its leadership in DLC technology which is essential in addressing the critical challenge of managing the heat arising from high-performance AI chips like the Nvidia GPUs. With the growing AI workloads, this DLC technology remains one of its major competitive advantage.

The other aspect that forms its MOAT is it's time to market advantage. One thing that I know Super Micro for is its rapid integration of the latest chip technologies, the most recent case being the integration of Nvidia’s Blackwell GPUs into ready to use server systems. It is an ability that stems from its partnership with leading chip makers like NVDA.

Why We’re Cautious

Although our bullish case is compelling, SMCI is faced with significant challenges that we feel warrant caution.

1. Governance Concerns

SMCI's FY2024 was marred by allegations of accounting irregularities, which were further compounded by its delayed filing of its 2024 financials. Although the company met the extended NASDAQ deadline of 25th February to file the financials, the ongoing Justice Department Investigation and shareholder class action lawsuit adds further uncertainty here.

2. Margin Pressure

Despite the strong topline growth, this company’s gross margins have been on a downward trend falling to as low as about 10% and despite a slight recovery, the downtrend appears to have resumed with the recent margin standing at about 11.8%.

The current margin is not only short of the company’s 5-year average of ~16% but also below the sector's median of ~50%. To us, this is a signal of higher cost of inputs or high production costs. The weak margin is a concern to us and without any clear turnaround strategy by the company this headwind could persist in the coming quarters leading to weak profitability.

1. Volatility And Valuation Risk

This stock has been a wild ride for investors, down more than 65% over the last year, and rebounding ~17% YTD. Given SMCI's 52-week range of 17.25-105.72 and its beta of 3.66, it is evident that this stock is very volatile, posing a major risk to investors. With this high beta, it follows that this stock is more than 3x volatile than the market which can lead to both big gains and losses depending on the trajectory.

In terms of valuation, we used the comparative PE approach where we compared the company to three of its major peers, as we’ve tabulated here.

When comparing SMCI's trailing twelve months PE ratio of 15.35x to its peers, it appears that this stock is trading at a premium. The peer average sits at 11.04x.

In estimating the stock’s price, we adopted two approaches whereby in the low end, we used the peer’s average trailing PE and the company’s trailing EPS of $2.28. This method yields a target price of $25.

For our high estimate, and given the company’s strong growth potential in the wake of scaled capacity and strong AI demand, we assumed a premium PE of 16x slightly above SMCI's forward PE. Using its estimated EPS of $2.59 for this year, this yields a target price of about $41.

Takeaway Message

There are of course causes for concern when it comes to SMCI, such as high volatility and a rather extreme beta. This means that this stock is a high-risk bet and that you need to be prepared for price volatility if you invest in SMCI. However, the company is leading the development of high-tech server racks for AI applications and boast not only with technological advancements but also with top partners such as Nvidia (NVDA). There is also the massive TAM that this company serves.

So, if you accept some uncertainty but want exposure to the booming AI market, and more specifically the hardware side of AI, this stock is a good buy. As such, we reiterate our Buy rating for the stock.

data.title: What U.S. Reciprocal Tariffs Mean For The Commodities Sector
data.description:
The U.S. has announced sweeping reciprocal tariffs. Daniel Ghali, Director, Commodities Strategy at TD Securities, breaks down what it could mean for oil, gold and the wider commodities sector.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: This new round of US tariffs weighing on the commodity space today, but as we go further out, are there any signs we should be watching for in those sectors? Joining us now to discuss all that, Daniel Ghali, Director for Commodity Strategy with TD Securities. Daniel, great to have you here.

Daniel Ghali: Yeah, thanks for having me, Greg.

Greg Bonnell: So we got a big piece of news. Obviously, the markets are reacting in a fairly significant way. What does it mean for commodities?

Daniel Ghali: Well, look, I think when something like this happens, markets shoot first, think later. So the information that we do have is that the tariff announcements were more significant than expected. The reprieve that Canada and Mexico did receive so far means that the tariff announcements are even more significant for inflation than they are for growth, but they're certainly significant. It's a significant growth shock as well.

Well, I think the commodities complex is trading completely in line with that. We're seeing broad-based selling activity across pretty much any commodity on your radar except for gold, which is the one outlier there. Within precious metals markets, I would say one of the really big stories over the last few months has been that the threat of tariffs has catalyzed a tidal wave of metal movement from everywhere in the world, but particularly from London, into the US. That relates to the market structure. But within one of the annexes tied to the executive order, they clarified that many commodities that we care about are exempt from these tariffs.

So what we've seen is the spread between US-based bullion contracts and other underlying physical metal completely collapsed over the course of this session. Interestingly, that hasn't had a very big impact on gold prices, but arguably, it may have an impact on other precious metals. You see, one of our thesis has been that these implications were unique for silver markets, where we were inadvertently draining the world's largest pool of metal in London. And the question now is, well, if bullion products are exempt, then will the metal that has already moved into the US flow back out?

And that is still up for debate. We think there're reasons to believe that it will flow back out at a much slower pace than it came in, and in which case you still have upside potential in some of these other precious metals. But the most interesting piece of price action today is certainly that gold seems untouchable.

Greg Bonnell: With gold well over $3,100 an ounce and making all these new highs, I think a lot of investors will look at that and say, given the times we're living in, can it move higher from here?

Daniel Ghali: Well, here's the most interesting piece about gold prices right now -- gold is overbought but under-owned. In contrast to what you might expect by looking simply at a chart of the price, we've actually seen very substantial liquidations from the speculative community over the course of the last months. Initially, this was the macro fund community. That's the group of investors who care about things like the Fed outlook, deficit, so on and so forth. They were holding an extreme position of size heading into the US election.

That's no longer the case today. In fact, today we think they hold less than they did in March of last year, where gold prices were trading at $2,000 an ounce. So really, what's driven those liquidations is unclear. It's possible that it's tied to constraints on risk budgets associated with the decline in equity prices as opposed to something that's specifically tied to gold prices itself. But what we do know is that in this moment of time, Western investors are actually under-positioned in gold. And the biggest buyers of gold over the last several weeks have been out of China. That's been a particularly strong story.

Greg Bonnell: Is this the central bank, or is that buying actually moving outside of the central bank?

Daniel Ghali: Well, the central bank is buying, but the new piece of information is that Chinese life insurance companies, for the first time ever, have been granted permission to purchase gold. That in and of itself -- we think Chinese life insurance companies have announced that they have already purchased some gold since they were allowed to. But we think that the narrative has bred animal spirits which are driving Chinese ETF fund flows. So that's really where the buying activity is coming from, is Chinese ETFs potentially tied to retail investing. But very substantial amounts of gold purchases through that channel.

Greg Bonnell: Interesting stuff there on the metals front. I want to talk to you about oil. When I first saw the move and West Texas Intermediate and Brent this morning, I thought, okay, well, tariffs, worries about global growth. But then I saw the OPEC headlines as well. What is going on with oil right now?

Daniel Ghali: Yeah, oil is under attack on several fronts. It's been our view that there's only a very narrow path to substantially higher oil prices this year. Truthfully, that right tail risk, as you might call it, is fairly fat, fatter than is currently being priced by markets. And it mostly relates to the prospect of increasing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East.

But for the time being, the combination of a growth shock, a decrease in supply risk premia tied to energy products being exempt from the tariffs, and OPEC announcing that they are bringing barrels back to the market at a faster rate than expected, this is an attack on several fronts on the oil price.

Original Post

data.title: Telix Pharmaceuticals: Building A Radiopharma Powerhouse
data.description:
Telix Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:TLX) is an interesting case study of execution within a dynamically developing radiopharmaceutical market. Though many investors associate the company primarily with the commercial success of its prostate cancer imaging agent, Illuccix, I believe a closer look reveals a company building a multi-faceted theranostic platform with potentially huge, underappreciated long term value. Recent financial performance is great, but deliberate pipeline advancement, strategic infrastructure investments and key regulatory wins are what drive my bullish thesis right now. Telix is building its next fleet on top of its first success.

Strong Financials Fuel Investment

Telix recently reported financial results showing disciplined execution (all figures I talk about below are in $AUD). Revenue of $783.2 million is up 56 percent from $502.5 million in 2023. This performance, which surpassed full-year guidance, was largely driven by continued market penetration and sales of Illuccix. A second consecutive year of profitable growth is commendable, especially considering the significant period of strategic investment.

The company is banking profits but reinvesting them wisely to drive further expansion. The $194.6 million investment is in line with guidance and demonstrates a clear focus on late-stage assets - the very products meant to diversify revenue streams beyond Illuccix. Simultaneously, Telix is advancing its global supply chain and product delivery infrastructure. All this talk is accompanied by concrete actions such as the acquisitions of ARTMS, IsoTherapeutics Group, and RLS USA, and the expansion of its Brussels South facility. All these moves contributed an additional $15.8 million in manufacturing and distribution investment towards reliable, scaled production, including GMP production at the Brussels facility by 2025.

Operational leverage is showing. Adjusted EBITDA jumped by 70 percent to $99.3 million despite significant investments underway. The FY2025 revenue guidance of $1.18 billion to $1.23 billion ($770 million to $800 million) also signals confidence going forward. Specifically, this guidance considers established Illuccix revenue in authorized markets and the 11 months of contribution from the acquired RLS business (less any double-counting of RLS Illuccix revenue). What is absolutely excluded is revenue generated from products for which marketing authorization is not yet issued - Gozellix, Pixclara and Zircaix. Taking this conservative approach inevitably includes potential upside when these products reach regulatory milestones and enter the market. Combining that with guidance for an expanded R&D investment range (20% to 25% higher than FY2024), the financial picture is one of leveraging current success to aggressively fund the next generation of growth drivers.

Drug Pipeline

Telix's strategy is based on theranostics - the combination of diagnostic imaging agents and targeted therapeutic agents targeting the same biological marker. This integrated approach enables precise patient selection, treatment monitoring and possibly more effective therapies. Its pipeline reflects this with multiple targets and indications.

The Prostate Specific membrane antigen remains the cornerstone. It is the commercial success story of Illuccix that provides the financial engine. On top of that, there is another gallium-68 PSMA-11 agent TLX007-CDx, now FDA-approved Gozellix (with additional advantages discussed below). The last of the PSMA theranostic pairs is TLX591, an agent in late-stage (Phase 3) development via the ProstACT GLOBAL trial with expanded recruitment in the U.S. This shows a clear path from diagnosis to treatment in their lead franchise.

Beyond prostate cancer, Telix is meeting other critical unmet needs. The target for kidney cancer is Carbonic Anhydrase IX, with Zircaix being a Phase 3 diagnostic candidate along with TLX250, a Phase 2 therapeutic. This is similar to the PSMA strategy and aims at a total theranostic solution.

Brain cancer imaging and therapy target L-type amino acid Transporter 1 with Pixclara as the Phase 3 diagnostic candidate and TLX101 as the Phase 2 therapeutic approach.

A different CD66 target is available for different indications. The Phase 3 diagnostic TLX66-CDx/Scintimum targets musculoskeletal infections (osteomyelitis) and TLX66 is a Phase 2 therapeutic candidate for bone marrow conditioning in hematology.

The pipeline is not static. Telix said it has received clinical proof of concept for its first alpha therapy candidate in prostate cancer, TLX592, and is exploring next-generation isotopes potentially with higher efficacy. This acquisition, targeting the FAP5-targeting theranostic further demonstrates proactive business development in the pipeline, notably in the urological franchise. This demonstrates a multimodal strategy employing various isotopes and targeting modalities (small molecules, antibodies) for diverse high-need indications.

ZOLAR Trial Expansion

Recent news indicates that Telix is looking to expand its theranostic approach beyond its current boundaries. The announcement of the first patient dosed in the Phase 1 ZOLAR trial for TLX300-CDx in advanced, metastatic soft tissue sarcomas is important. Olaratumab, originally developed by Eli Lilly and Company, targets platelet-derived growth factor receptor alpha, a protein often over-expressed in STS. Telix holds exclusive worldwide rights to develop and commercialize radiolabelled forms.

The ZOLAR trial is a first-in-human, proof-of-concept PET study to establish the optimal dose for the diagnostic agent TLX300-CDx. But in keeping with Telix's theranostic philosophy, the study goes further. The dosimetry and target expression data collected in this imaging study should guide the selection of an appropriate therapeutic radionuclide for a future STS treatment candidate targeting PDGFR alpha. That move represents Telix's strategy to use diagnostic imaging to de-risk and guide the development of a paired therapeutic, potentially creating an entirely new oncology vertical for the company.

Gozellix FDA Approval Impact

The most dramatic recent development is Gozellix, Telix's next-generation PSMA-PET imaging agent for prostate cancer. Gozellix is radiolabeled with gallium-68 and targets the same patient populations as Illuccix - men with suspected metastasis candidates for first-definitive therapy and suspected recurrence based on increasing PSA levels.

It may seem redundant to launch another gallium-68 PSMA agent, but Gozellix has unique and commercially important advantages. Its novel formulation permits a shelf life of up to six hours and a wider distribution radius than other gallium-based imaging products. This isn't some technical tidbit; this is a market access unlocker. The ability to ship more reliably farther from the production site means Gozellix may be able to service PET imaging centers not accessible by any other imaging provider. It directly fills a critical unmet need by making accurate and useful advanced gallium-based imaging accessible to more patients in the United States. Moreover, the new formulation allows for more scalable production which may increase productivity, scheduling flexibility & throughput in busy scanning clinics.

Going forward, Gozellix hopes to build on Illuccix by offering choice to customers and patients. US PSMA-PET imaging has become the gold standard of care, but market penetration is still low - only a small fraction of the estimated 3.4 million men with prostate cancer have had this type of precision medicine scan. A key barrier is access. Telix feels that this is directly addressed in Gozellix. Further fueling this potential is the expectation that Gozellix will receive full reimbursement - possibly with low or no patient co-insurance. It could thus significantly enlarge its reach, especially among underserved patient populations - a real driver for growth that has not yet been reflected in the company's conservative forward guidance.

Risks and Competition

Clinical trial risk remains paramount; even with promising data, late-stage assets such as Zircaix, Pixclara, TLX591 and others must pass Phase 3 trials and regulatory approval. Delayed or negative trials are always possible. Commercialization risk also exists; launching new products such as Gozellix and achieving desired market uptake requires strong sales and marketing execution, as well as navigating complex reimbursement landscapes for future pipeline candidates.

Execution risk extends to operations. It will take some management to integrate the acquired businesses (ARTMS, IsoTherapeutics Group, RLS USA) and bring the expanded Brussels facility online for GMP production by 2025. Deficiencies or delays in scaling manufacturing could affect growth projections.

Radiopharmaceutical competition is becoming more intense. Establishment players such as Novartis (NVS) with Pluvicto and Locametz have resources, and numerous other companies are developing diagnostics and therapeutics targeting similar pathways, such as PSMA, so Telix has to differentiate with innovation, such as Gozellix's formulation or new targets like PDGFR alpha, efficient manufacturing, and strong commercial execution. Current revenue is heavily concentrated on Illuccix, so successful pipeline diversification is essential for long-term de-risking, though this takes time and capital.

Valuation and Market Size

Telix’s valuation, like many biotechs, is not accurately captured by conventional metrics. The potential of a radiopharma company lies not in trailing profits but in the size of its addressable markets. Let's take prostate cancer: with over 1.4 million new worldwide cases per year and about half eligible for advanced imaging, Telix targets around 700,000 patients a year. That equates to nearly $500 million in annual revenue potential at 25% penetration and $2,800 per scan - before you add Gozellix's wider reach or therapeutic follow-ons like TLX591.

The upside is substantial for kidney and brain cancers. Renal cell carcinoma accounts for 430,000 new global cases per year. If Telix applies its theranostic model here, it would have a market worth over $1 billion, especially for high-value therapeutic agents. Less common brain tumors like gliomas warrant premium pricing - treatment regimens with TLX101 could cost $20,000-$40,000 per patient. With the addition of CD66-targeted applications in bone marrow conditioning and infections, where treatment costs can exceed $50,000, the TAM reaches well beyond $10 billion.

Even modest market shares across these areas could outstrip revenues today. No, Telix does not need blockbuster hits - just steady approvals and smart execution to unlock multi-billion dollar opportunities.

Future Outlook

The risks aside, I think Telix Pharmaceuticals makes a compelling investment case. The company has demonstrated excellent commercial execution with Illuccix and generated substantial revenue and profit. More important, Telix is putting that success back into a deep, diversified pipeline based on theranostic principles. Recent FDA approval of Gozellix represents a validation and a near-term catalyst that could significantly broaden market access.

Its deliberate expansion of manufacturing and supply chain capabilities through acquisitions and facility build-outs demonstrates foresight and commitment to scaling operations globally. Several shots on goal are provided by the pipeline, including late-stage assets in key oncology indications and exploratory efforts in novel areas such as STS and alpha therapy.

Currently the financial guidance is conservative and excludes potential contributions from new approved or near approval products. If this pipeline matures and Gozellix picks up, I see strong opportunity for revenue diversification and continued strong growth. While competition and execution risks must be monitored, Telix's focused strategy, track record, and recent positive developments point to a strong future in the radiopharmaceutical space. If you have a long-term view and want exposure to this high-growth sector, Telix is worth considering.

data.title: New U.S. Tariffs: What It Means For Canada
data.description:
The U.S. has unveiled its reciprocal tariff plan. Derek Burleton, Deputy Chief Economist, TD looks at the potential impact for Canada’s economy, interest rates and the future of global trade

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: The United States has unveiled a fresh round of tariffs impacting its global trading partners. And while Canada escaped those reciprocal tariffs, we're not totally unscathed. Joining us now to discuss is Derek Burleton, VP and Deputy Chief Economist with TD Economics. Derek, thanks so much for taking the time to talk with us.

Derek Burleton: My pleasure.

Greg Bonnell: So, as I said, we are watching, obviously, the developments in the Rose Garden live. We're saying, at first blush, like, where's Canada on that list? It seems we have escaped this latest round. But we already had some tariffs in place. What does it mean for the Canadian economy? Does it change our view of where we might be headed?

Derek Burleton: Well, yeah. Best deal in a bad deal. But I was braced for more tariffs yesterday. We didn't see them -- no net new. But we're still dealing with pre-existing tariffs -- steel and aluminum on the auto sector.

But I think even more, it's the uncertainty. We're hearing news that Chrysler is going to shut down for a couple of weeks in Windsor assembly, given the uncertainty around tariffs. So you're getting impacts. But for us, as forecasters, we had built a lot of that into our March forecast out a few weeks ago. We actually had a steeper tariff assumption than looks like it's going to happen, at least for now.

We had 12.5% average tariff rate on Canada. Based on yesterday's announcement, what we know, that's looking heavy. But then I still worry about further tariffs on copper and some of the other areas. So, for the most part, it looks like we avoided the worst. I worry about uncertainty impacts. It's certainly going to slow growth in Canada, and worried about potential escalation as well going forward.

Greg Bonnell: I know before yesterday as well that your team had done work on the potential government supports and what they could look like. Anything about what's happening right now that has changed your mind on that front?

Derek Burleton: Well, the two things I'm watching, I'm watching retaliation. And some of the early word we're getting on retaliation is it's going to be very targeted on the US auto sector from Canada's perspective. So we'll see how that rolls over in the United States, but hoping that's okay. But then that was expected.

But in terms of fiscal response, if anything, we will get quite an offset. You get an offset from a number of areas. You get an offset from lower interest rates. Canada's been very quick to cut rates.

You get an offset from a weak-ish Canadian dollar, which, actually, as of right now, is showing resilience. But we do have a soft Canadian dollar in our forecast. But as well, the fiscal response looks like it's going to exceed our expectations.

We had 1% of GDP, largely to support impacts from the tariffs. Provincially, we're already looking at that when you build in a likely Ontario response in its budget. Plus, you have all the election measures federally. And I think it's going to give the economy a bit of an offsetting lift. So, hopefully, we avoid kind of a significant recession in Canada. We might even get by with stagnation, even some mild growth this year.

Greg Bonnell: How much further can the Bank of Canada go? They have been delivering those rate cuts. We did have inflation back down to target. It's picked up a little bit. How much room do they have for the rest of this year to give more rate relief?

Derek Burleton: Well, they don't have a lot of room, but that's under a scenario where the economy doesn't encounter very, very steep headwinds, as we're not expecting at the moment. But all that to say, we had two cuts in our forecast.

This spring may be a bit delayed, because I think Bank of Canada is going to be looking at some upward inflation impact from Canadian tariffs on the US. They've got to walk the tightrope there with some of the weakening economy. I think, certainly, by the late spring, it's going to have enough evidence that the economy is weakening to cut rates on two more occasions.

And it'll stop there provided that, again, we avoid weaker downward scenarios that we're not currently embedding. So I think two cuts. That'll get short rates down to 2.25%, and I think should be enough to provide some of the underlying support to keep the economy from its head submerging too much.

Greg Bonnell: Even though we are in this tariff environment, of course, as goes the US economy, often goes the Canadian economy. When we take a look at the actions that have been taken by the Trump administration, what are your thoughts on the US economy?

Derek Burleton: Well, that it's weaker. But we had had an expectation for the US that was quite upbeat for this year. The trend was your friend there. The economy doing well quarter after quarter. We had assumed slowdown, but nothing too sharp -- something closer to trend 2%. Now with yesterday's announcement, we're certainly looking at a deep downgrade to that growth outlook -- at least a half a point.

Still positive for 2025, but 2026 could also get marked down. So certainly, a much weaker US forecast just based on deeper tariffs and uncertainty. And the job numbers tomorrow will be very key to give us a better sense of starting position. So we'll see what those numbers are like. But, overall, yes, we're looking at taking a cleaver to the US outlook in our next forecast.

And that won't help Canada. That's one of those, along with weaker commodity prices, you got to factor that into any Canadian equation. But with fiscal stimulus and the tariffs not being as steep as some had expected, I think there will be some offsets there.

Greg Bonnell: If the aim here of the US administration is to reshore, to bring manufacturing back to their country, what does that actually mean in terms of cost, timeline?

Derek Burleton: Well, I think the end goal is having the US supply its own needs as much as possible, and so a major shift, obviously, from the status quo -- several decades of globalization. How long that takes, I think there's certainly limits in the near term. You might get some increased capacity in the auto sector, for example. But, again, you can't just move over quickly. Auto plants are designed to supply certain vehicles. It would cost billions of dollars to retool. And then you've got labor shortages. And that's going to be, I think, longer term, a bit of an issue. So we'll see. I think the two- to three-year time frame is often given is when you can get significant investment response.

We, in our commentary, note that, by 2027, you could get 500,000 more jobs in US manufacturing, which, relative to the size of the economy, isn't massive. And the cost per job, given the cost of tariffs, would equate to about $1 million a job. So, given that robots and everything will be housed in a lot of those manufacturing facilities, there's certainly limits there.

Greg Bonnell: Before I let you go, Derek, I want to ask you, this reordering, really, of the global economy like you're talking about, what do we think about the global economy? Is there still a lot of dominoes to fall before we can figure out what the wide-ranging effects are?

Derek Burleton: Yes. We're talking from a very early stage. We've got to see retaliation. I worry about, for example, Europe retaliating on US services, intellectual property, digital service tax. That could take this trade war to a completely different level, given the US have threatened tit-for-tat responses to retaliations.

So a lot of potential headwinds there. Financial markets could be another headwind. So we have to sit back, monitor in the coming days. Hopefully, some cooler minds will prevail. And in a best case, you get trade deals with the US. You get a US administration that wants to shift to tax cuts, kind of the next stage of its agenda. And hopefully, we will see a number of deals fall in the coming weeks and months. And that'll kind of soothe a little bit of the nervousness right now.

Original Post

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook - FOMC Minutes, Tariff Developments And Inflation Ahead
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Week in review: Liberation day tariffs send markets tumbling

A bloodbath week for US Equity markets and markets as a whole has finally come to an end. Market sentiment soured after President Trump revealed his tariff proposals, which weighed on global growth expectations and sent US stocks tumbling.

On Thursday, hedge funds sold stocks at their highest single day amount since 2010. At the same time, retail investors bought $4.7 billion worth of stocks, the most in over a decade. Friday, the S&P 500 lost another $1.5 trillion in market value, bringing the total two-day losses to $3.5 trillion.

Friday's losses came about as China announced it will retaliate against U.S. President Donald Trump by imposing an extra 34% tariff on U.S. goods. This move intensifies the trade war, worrying investors and raising fears of a possible recession.

Source: LSEG, US Census Bureau

Developments this week saw investment bank J.P. Morgan increase their estimates to 60% that the global economy would enter a recession by year-end, up from 40% previously.

Big tech stocks dropped, pushing the Nasdaq closer to a bear market. Companies that rely heavily on China and Taiwan for manufacturing were hit hard, with Apple (AAPL) falling 6.4% and Nvidia (NVDA) dropping 7.7%.

The Nasdaq was set to close more than 20% below its record high from December.

It appears that market participants are now looking to the Federal Reserve and Chair Jerome Powell to be the saviour.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell told a business journalists' conference on Friday that the tariffs were "bigger than expected" and increased the risk of higher inflation and slower economic growth.

The Fed plans to wait for more data before deciding on monetary policy but will focus on keeping inflation under control if Trump's tariffs lead to lasting price increases, Powell said.

He didn’t directly comment on the U.S. stock market selloff but admitted that uncertainty is causing businesses to delay decisions. "People are just waiting for clarity," Powell said. "I can't say when it will clear up, but it eventually will."

On the FX front, The U.S. dollar strengthened against major currencies like the euro and yen on Friday after Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell discussed the impact of bigger-than-expected U.S. tariffs and took a cautious approach to future rate cuts.

The Aussie Dollar lost ground on Friday and hit five-year lows against the greenback after China announced its tariff stance and retaliation. The Swiss Franc and the Japanese Yen both benefitted this week from safe haven appeal, both gaining significant ground across the board.

Commodities were interesting to observe this week. Gold prices rose immediately after the tariff announcement but experienced a significant selloff since then. Gold reached a high of 3167 before the selloff saw prices touch a low of 3015 on Friday. The only explanation that I could draw was significant profit taking and the possibility that the tariffs were largely priced in.

Oil prices struggled this week as OPEC + announced further output increases. That coupled with tariff fears sent oil prices to their lowest level since 2021. Friday's losses were around 8%.

Heading into next week and the tariff picture is far from clear. Market participants will do well to focus on potential deals between the US and other countries, which could help sentiment improve. However, if no deals materialize and countries follow the China approach, markets could be in for another week of pain.

The week ahead: Markets eye tariff developments and the FOMC minutes

The upcoming week will focus on U.S. President Donald Trump's plans for new tariffs. Alongside this, markets will also watch U.S. jobs data, an Australian central bank meeting, and a key eurozone inflation report.

Asia Pacific Markets
